<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: routing-heads-and-specialist-cores
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/routing-heads-and-specialist-cores.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/routing-heads-and-specialist-cores.qmd@7516e20c2
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Routing Heads and Specialist Cores

A system with many abilities does not become governed by having all of them
available at once. It becomes governed when it can decide which ability is
allowed to act, what it is allowed to see, what tools it may use, how ready it
is for this work, and where responsibility goes when the route fails.

That is the routing problem. It is not only a question of speed or cost. It is
the moment where capability becomes authority. A specialist may be the best
semantic fit and still be too broad, too stale, too expensive, too private, too
untested, or too dangerous for the task in front of it.

The ASI Stack treats a route as a lease. A route temporarily grants a bounded
specialist the context, tools, budget, verifier, fallback, and residual burden
needed for one piece of work. The lease expires. The record remains. A
governed router must remember not only why one specialist was selected, but
also why other plausible specialists were refused.

This is where modular intelligence stops being a pile of tools and becomes a
control surface.

## Problem

Part II built the surfaces a system can route through: plans, contracts,
context, claim ledgers, proof-carrying claims, execution jobs, audit logs,
runtime adapters, and procedural memory. Once those surfaces exist, the next
problem is selection under constraint.

The stack cannot send every task to one general cognitive core and still claim
local memory, local authority, readiness-aware selection, or clean rollback.
The more work the system accepts, the more it needs a way to say: this bounded
capability may act here, under this evidence state, with this authority subset,
against this fallback.

The Octopus Router source gives the chapter its head-and-arms vocabulary: a
small resident router selects dynamically loaded specialist arms. RMI adds the
ratchet: attempts, logs, residuals, benchmark pressure, regression
preservation, and lifecycle discipline. Project Theseus adds local-first
implementation pressure around registries, task allowlists, operator boards,
and visible safety gates. MoECOT remains runtime-reference context only; its
source and artifacts have not been reproduced here.

The hard part is refusing the plausible route. A specialist can look relevant
while missing the necessary authority, readiness, context, budget, proof
boundary, or fallback. A routing chapter that only celebrates successful
selection would miss the governance problem. The useful record is the one that
preserves rejected candidates and the reasons they were rejected.

## Why Ordinary Routing Fails

Ordinary routing often optimizes the wrong thing. A load balancer may ask
which worker is available. A model router may ask which model gives the best
quality per dollar. A tool-using agent may ask which tool name appears to fit
the prompt. Those are useful questions, but they are not enough for governed
intelligence.

A route can mutate an artifact, read a memory, call a tool, use a private
context, consult a proof checker, spend a budget, or advise a high-impact
decision. That makes routing an authority decision. The router needs a
registry and a receipt, not only a confidence score.

External routing work is still the right baseline. Sparse MoE, GShard, Switch
Transformers, Expert Choice routing, Mixtral, the MoE survey literature,
FrugalGPT, Hybrid LLM, and RouteLLM all make conditional computation,
capacity, learned routing, and cost-quality tradeoffs explicit. The ASI Stack
does not claim to outperform those systems here. It borrows the comparison
vocabulary and adds a different obligation: the route must carry authority,
readiness, fallback, residuals, and non-claims.

Prompt-only orchestration fails this boundary most often. It can choose a tool
or model and produce a good answer, while leaving no durable explanation of
the route. Later, the system cannot reconstruct which candidates were rejected,
which authority was denied, which context lease was granted, which fallback was
available, or which residual was left behind. That is how a clever dispatcher
becomes an ungoverned capability transfer.

## Core Claim

ASI scales through a lightweight routing head that selects bounded specialist
cores with local tools, memory, and authority. In the live AI/research book,
this claim remains a design rationale supported at `argument`.

The current evidence supports the chapter as architecture, not as a measured
router. The assigned sources justify discussion of router heads, specialist
arms, permission envelopes, memory routing, residual-aware routing, tool
cards, arm lifecycle, and operator-visible routing. The repository also has
public schemas, fixtures, two finite Lean hooks, and synthetic
readiness/residual checks that touch authority-bounded and fallback routing.

Those artifacts do not prove routing accuracy, learned-router quality,
specialist adequacy, deployed authority enforcement, route-quality dominance,
MoECOT runtime behavior, current Theseus runtime behavior, or safety. The
point is to make the route inspectable before claiming it works.

## Mechanism

The mechanism has three parts: a specialist registry, a routing decision
record, and a route receipt.

The registry describes the standing maximum envelope of a specialist. It says
what the specialist can do, who owns it, what memory and tools it may lease,
what authority ceiling applies, what it costs, what readiness state it is in,
what quality predicates are relevant, what fallback routes exist, and what the
record does not claim.

The routing decision record describes one act of selection. It begins with a
capability request from a plan or job. It lists candidate specialists. It names
the selected specialist and the rejected candidates. It records the route
shape, the authority check, the granted authority subset, the denied authority,
the readiness check, the context lease, the tool lease, the verifier
requirement, the budget, the expiry, the fallback route, and the residual
owner.

The receipt is the portable artifact. It lets later layers reconstruct why the
system chose this specialist now, why it did not choose another one, and what
obligations survived the route.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Request["Capability request"] --> Registry["Specialist registry"]
  Registry --> Fit["Capability fit"]
  Fit --> Authority["Authority envelope"]
  Authority --> Ready["Readiness state"]
  Ready --> Cost["Cost and quality"]
  Cost --> Decision{"Adequate route?"}
  Decision -- "yes" --> Lease["Task-local route lease"]
  Decision -- "no" --> Fallback["Fallback, review, or residual"]
  Lease --> Receipt["Route receipt"]
  Fallback --> Receipt
  Receipt --> Ledger["Evidence and residual ledger"]
```

The routing head should stay small. Its work is to select, load, coordinate,
verify, and log. Specialist cores carry bounded local capability: tools,
memory, benchmarks, residuals, permissions, runtime tier, and verification
contracts. A core may be a model, proof checker, retrieval lane, planning
compiler, human review lane, mathematical substrate, tool stack, or composite
worker. What matters is not the form of the core. What matters is that the
route grants only the task-local subset it is allowed to use.

The router also needs route shapes. Some tasks need one specialist. Some need
sequential routing, where one specialist prepares work for another. Some need
parallel routing, where independent specialists produce comparable outputs.
Some need debate or adjudication because disagreement is useful evidence. Some
need verification routing, where one core checks another. Some need reflex or
failsafe routes for known safety-critical cases. All of these shapes still
need the same receipt discipline.

MoECOT is the deferred runtime-crosswalk case. The consolidation roadmap may
later fold the standalone MoECOT runtime chapter into this chapter as a named
runtime section if review accepts that move. This curated reader pass does not
execute that fold. It treats MoECOT as source-note runtime context for compact
orchestration, specialist lanes, control-plane gates, ledgers, replay refs,
handoffs, promotion blockers, and residuals. No MoECOT replay, benchmark,
route log, or deployment behavior is claimed.

## Interfaces

Routing sits between plans and execution. Planning requests capabilities, not
personalities. Governance bounds authority. Context systems lease memory and
context. Readiness gates decide whether a specialist is ordinary, canary,
diagnostic, quarantined, or unavailable. Artifact graphs preserve the route so
downstream claims can trace where an output came from.

The Specialist Registry Record is the interface for standing specialist
identity: specialist ID, registry epoch, owner, capabilities, authority
envelope, memory lease policy, tool permissions, runtime tier, cost profile,
readiness state, quality predicates, fallback routes, residual references,
evidence references, route limitations, and non-claims.

The Routing Decision Record is the interface for one route: decision ID, task
ID, capability request, candidate specialists, selected specialist, rejected
candidates, non-selection evidence, route shape, route receipt, authority
check, granted authority subset, denied authority, readiness check, context
lease, tool lease, verifier requirement, budget, expiry, cost-quality reason,
fallback route, residual owner, residuals, ledger references, and non-claims.

The record is intentionally dense because the failure is usually a missing
field. If the system cannot say what authority was denied, a later reviewer may
assume it was granted. If it cannot say which specialists were rejected, a
later optimizer may treat the chosen path as obviously best. If it cannot say
who owns the residual, the failed route becomes someone else's invisible work.

## Invariants

Specialist authority is local. A specialist's existence does not grant it
global permission. The registry declares a maximum envelope, and the route
grants a smaller task-local lease.

Selection and authorization are different predicates. A specialist can be the
best semantic match and still be blocked by authority, readiness, context,
budget, or fallback requirements.

The router should prefer the least capable adequate route. It should not call
a broader, stronger, or more privileged specialist unless task risk, context
demand, verifier need, or fallback policy justifies that extra power.

Readiness failures do not become silent execution. They route to fallback,
review, quarantine, diagnostic use, or residual escrow.

Rejected candidates matter. Non-selection evidence is part of the learning
surface, because future routing quality depends on knowing what the system did
not use and why.

The current Lean hooks cover only a small finite-record version of this
boundary: a selected route satisfies authority and readiness, and failed
readiness routes to fallback or residual rather than promotion. They do not
prove real router behavior, learned selection, specialist quality, or runtime
enforcement.

## Failure Modes

Wrong specialist selection should become a residual and a future benchmark
case, not a quiet mistake.

Router overconfidence turns a plausible match into a permission grant. The
right response is an authority or readiness block, not a more fluent answer.

Authority leak happens when a specialist receives broader memory, tools, or
mutation rights than the task required. This is a governance incident, not a
mere routing inefficiency.

Specialist proliferation creates a registry full of stale, redundant, or
unowned cores. RMI's lifecycle language matters here: specialists need split,
merge, retire, quarantine, and retrain paths.

Composition hallucination appears when multiple specialists return partial
answers and the head fuses them into a confident output without preserving
conflict, uncertainty, or verification duty.

Route laundering is the chapter's sharpest failure. A broad specialist is used
because it is convenient. The output succeeds. That success is then treated as
evidence that broad authority was justified, even though the route never proved
that narrower specialists were inadequate. A route receipt should make that
move visible.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is not a learned router. It is a registry
schema, a routing decision schema, and a small fixture suite that makes route
decisions inspectable.

The first useful route suite should include one least-capable adequate route,
one rejected overprivileged specialist, one missing-readiness fallback, one
expired context lease, and one failed composition with a named residual owner.
That is enough to test whether the record can carry the obligation.

The current repository now has this record-level minimum. The specialist
registry and routing decision schemas validate record shape. The synthetic
routing decision lease harness checks three valid and seven expected-invalid
route packets for least-capable adequate selection, overprivileged rejection,
authority-envelope containment, missing-readiness fallback, expired-lease
residualization, rejected-candidate evidence, residual ownership, selected
specialist registration, and MoECOT source-boundary non-claims.

The readiness/residual gate harness adds a second synthetic surface for
authority-bounded routing and fallback behavior across route, gate, and
replacement records. `AsiStackProofs.Routing` models finite route decisions
that preserve request, registry, authority, readiness, lease, cost-quality,
rejected-candidate, residual-owner, and non-claim boundaries. The folded
MoECOT slice adds orchestration fixture validation plus finite
source-boundary predicates so runtime-branding claims cannot promote above
`argument` without readiness, regression, replay, and source-state evidence.

This is a contract surface, not a deployed router. It does not measure routing
accuracy, train a learned router, enforce runtime authority, prove specialist
adequacy, run MoECOT, replay Theseus, benchmark orchestration, prove route
quality, or promote the chapter claim.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a governed capability market inside the stack. It is
not a larger tool picker. It is a routing control plane that can choose among
specialists, proof checkers, retrieval lanes, local runtime cores, human
review, compression systems, mathematical substrates, and fallback routes
while preserving the authority, evidence, and residual obligations attached to
that choice.

In that mature system, every specialist has a capability card, readiness
state, authority envelope, memory and tool lease policy, cost and latency
profile, proof or verifier requirements, fallback routes, retirement criteria,
and non-claims. Every route produces a receipt. Every receipt can feed
benchmark ratchets, residual escrow, artifact graphs, claim ledgers, and
policy updates.

The strategic gain is not simply cheaper inference. It is safer delegation
under evidence. A system can call a small specialist because the small
specialist is enough. It can call a human because the route demands human
judgment. It can call a proof checker because the claim requires a proof
boundary. It can refuse all available specialists because the authority,
readiness, or fallback conditions are not satisfied.

That is the beyond-state-of-the-art target. It remains unvalidated here. The
chapter names the product-level endpoint so implementation work has a precise
shape, not so the book can claim the endpoint already exists.

## Summary

Routing makes modular intelligence governable only when it is recorded as a
bounded decision. The head selects. The specialist acts inside a local
envelope. Evidence updates readiness. Rejections shape future policy.
Residuals become visible work.

The present repository stops before route-quality evidence. It has source
mappings, external comparators, schemas, finite Lean predicates, synthetic
readiness/residual checks, and the synthetic routing decision lease harness.
It does not have a measured specialist router, learned router, runtime
enforcement result, MoECOT replay, orchestration benchmark, current Theseus
runtime rerun, or support-state promotion.

A route should answer a simple question: why this specialist, under this
authority, now? If that answer cannot be reconstructed, the router is not yet
a governed control surface.

## Handoff

Selection is not qualification. Routing can nominate a specialist, but
Readiness Gates, Residual Escrow, and Quarantine decides whether that
specialist is ordinary, canary, diagnostic, quarantined, retired, or blocked.
The next chapter gives route candidates a lifecycle so promising capability
does not become permission before the evidence is ready.
